ScheduleClaimStatus.description: One claim recorded against a pipeline.

logical_ts.description: The occurrence this claim belongs to. Null for a webhook demand, whose claim key carries a minted id rather than an instant.

outcome.description: The terminal outcome, when the claim is `completed`.

submission_id.description: The submission id — the join key to `GET /api/v1/jobs/{id}` and to run history.

heartbeat_age_seconds.description: Age of the lock's heartbeat in seconds, when one is readable.

state.description: Lock state. Note `free` is the normal steady state — see [`TickLockState`].

awaiting_first_anchor.description: True when no tick has recorded a cron anchor yet. The first tick records the anchor *without* firing, so `next_fire_at` is not yet projectable — which is a different thing from "this will never fire".

catchup.description: Catch-up policy: `latest` (fire the newest missed occurrence) or `skip` (jump the anchor forward, firing none of them).

claims.description: Claims recorded for this pipeline, newest occurrence first.

config_error.description: The config error making this pipeline unschedulable, when its schedule cannot be resolved. The reconciler fails closed and never fires it, so this is why an otherwise-configured pipeline is silent.

consecutive_failures.description: Consecutive failures driving the backoff ladder.

cron.description: The cron expression, when one is configured.

enabled.description: False when the schedule declares `enabled = false`.

freshness_budget_seconds.description: The resolved freshness budget in seconds, when that trigger is active.

last_evaluated_at.description: When the reconciler last evaluated this pipeline's demand. Staleness here — not the tick lock — is what indicates a dead timer.

last_fire_logical_ts.description: The logical timestamp of the last occurrence that fired.

next_fire_at.description:
When the cron is next expected to fire.

**A timestamp in the past means overdue.** The projection is anchored on the stored cursor, not on the clock, so a stalled timer reports the slot it missed rather than a healthy-looking future one. Null for `after`/`freshness` schedules, which have no projectable instant.

throttle.description: The active backoff window, when the pipeline is throttled.

ScheduleStatusCounts.description: Roll-up counts over the reported pipelines.

config_errors.description: How many declare a schedule that cannot be resolved.

enabled.description: Of those, how many are enabled.

in_flight.description: How many have a `submitted` claim — a run believed to be in flight.

overdue.description: How many have a `next_fire_at` in the past (overdue).

scheduled.description: Pipelines declaring a schedule (the length of `pipelines`).

throttled.description: How many are currently inside a backoff window.

ScheduleThrottleKind.description: Why a demand is currently suppressed.

oneOf.description: The fixed backoff applied after a partial run.

kind.description: Which backoff is suppressing the demand.

resume_at.description: When the demand becomes eligible again.

TickLockState.description:
Tick-lock states.

**`free` is the normal steady state.** The lock is held only for the duration of a tick, and a tick is short relative to the poll interval, so a perfectly healthy scheduler reports `free` on almost every request. It does not mean "no scheduler is running" — for that, compare `last_evaluated_at` against the schedule's cadence.

oneOf.description: No lock file exists — no tick has ever run in this project.
Not currently held. The normal state between ticks.
Held by a live reconciler (fresh heartbeat) — a tick is in progress.
Held with a heartbeat stale past the takeover window: a wedged reconciler. The next tick overrides the lock and proceeds.

description:
A read-only snapshot of the scheduler: what is scheduled, when each pipeline last evaluated and fired, when it is next expected to, what is claimed in flight, and whether a reconciler holds the tick lock.

API-only (`GET /api/v1/schedule`), so it carries no `version`/`command` preamble — it mirrors [`MetaOutput`] and `JobStatus`, not the CLI-backed [`TickOutput`].

This is a **status read, not an evaluation**: it reports stored cursors, claims and lock state without deciding what is due. `rocky tick --dry-run` is the evaluation. Keeping the two apart makes this endpoint side-effect free and `O(pipelines)` rather than `O(runs × pipelines)`.

now.description: The instant this snapshot was taken. Every projection below is relative to it.

pipelines.description: Every schedulable pipeline declaring a `[schedule]`, sorted by name. Load pipelines are excluded — they are not schedulable (a scheduled load would re-ingest every discovered file), so reporting one as scheduled would promise a fire that can never happen.

tick_lock.description: Whether a reconciler currently holds the tick lock.

timezone.description: The project timezone cron occurrences resolve in.

title: ScheduleStatusOutput
